Title: Synergy between the anthocyanin and RDR6/ SGS3/DCL4 siRNA pathways expose hidden features of Arabidopsis carbon metabolism

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: vacuole of vegetative tissues for anthocyanins; seed coat for proanthocyanidins
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: anthocyanins are visual stress/metabolic status pigments; proanthocyanidins are seed coat pigments
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: TT19 is essential for normal anthocyanin accumulation in seedlings and proanthocyanidin pigmentation in seed coat; loss causes severe anthocyanin deficiency but can be partially bypassed by rdr6/sgs3/dcl4 mutations.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: EMS suppressor screen in tt19-8; mutant phenotyping; double mutant analysis; anthocyanin quantification; flavonoid profiling
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. tt19-8 seedlings accumulate only about 10–11% of wild-type anthocyanin under AIC. 2. Mutations in RDR6, SGS3, or DCL4 suppress the tt19 anthocyanin defect in vegetative tissues but not the seed-coat PA defect. 3. If enough anthocyanins are produced, they can accumulate even in the absence of TT19, indicating TT19 is important but not absolutely required when pathway flux is strongly increased.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: anthocyanin inductive conditions (3% sucrose, strong light); soil-grown developmental stages
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq GSE136680; small RNA-seq GSE136901; whole-genome resequencing PRJNA562949; untargeted metabolomics; LC-MS/MS flavonoid profiling
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss causes strong anthocyanin deficiency in seedlings and reduced seed coat proanthocyanidins.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important target for improving stable anthocyanin accumulation/sequestration, but this study shows sequestration defects may be partially compensated by increasing pathway flux.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: TT19 is proposed to bind and stabilize anthocyanins and participate in vacuolar sequestration; exact mechanism remains unresolved.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: pigments reporting stress and metabolic status
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PAP1 overexpression increases anthocyanins in wild type but is insufficient to restore anthocyanin accumulation in tt19; increased PAP1 is necessary but not sufficient in suppressor lines.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: genetic cross with PAP1-D; RT-qPCR; anthocyanin quantification
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PAP1-D raises PAP1 mRNA about fivefold and increases anthocyanin about 60% in wild type. 2. tt19-8 PAP1-D fails to accumulate more anthocyanins than tt19-8 despite high PAP1 transcript. 3. Therefore PAP1 upregulation alone cannot bypass TT19 loss, distinguishing transcriptional pull from the stronger carbon-push mechanism in suppressor lines.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Induced by high light, high sucrose, senescence, and anthocyanin-inducing conditions
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: high in PAP1-D and elevated in rdr6/sgs3/dcl4-related suppressor backgrounds
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: four copies of CaMV35S promoter in PAP1-D, constitutive
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: high light; high sucrose; anthocyanin inductive conditions
Common_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: PAP1 mRNA is post-transcriptionally controlled by TAS4-siRNA81 via the RDR6-SGS3-DCL4 pathway
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression alone gives only modest pathway pull and does not rescue sequestration-defective tt19.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for increasing anthocyanin production, but stacking with sequestration/transport solutions or carbon-flux engineering is needed for stronger accumulation.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as necessary but clearly insufficient for suppressor phenotype.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AtGSTF5 is highly upregulated in suppressor lines but is neither sufficient nor necessary to restore anthocyanin accumulation in tt19 backgrounds.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq expression analysis; transgenic overexpression of WT and siRNA-insensitive optimized CDS; CRISPR/Cas9 knockout in suppressor backgrounds; anthocyanin phenotyping
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtGSTF5 transcript strongly increases in tt19 suppressor lines. 2. Overexpressing AtGSTF5 in tt19-8 does not complement anthocyanin deficiency. 3. Editing AtGSTF5 in tt19 suppressor backgrounds does not reduce restored anthocyanin levels. 4. Therefore AtGSTF5 is unlikely to mediate anthocyanin stabilization/vacuolar sequestration in this context.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: normally very low in wild type and tt19-8; elevated in suppressor lines
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV35S promoter, constitutive, for overexpression constructs
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Not a useful standalone target to rescue anthocyanin sequestration defects in tt19.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Proposed due to similarity to TT19 and possible siRNA targeting, but experimentally ruled out.

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: tt19-8 S2 and other suppressor-related analyses
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: pigment accumulation coupled to carbon allocation
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: SUS4 mRNA increases in suppressor lines and is implicated as a contributor to enhanced sugar catabolism supplying extra carbon for flavonoid biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq; small RNA-seq mapping; integrative inference from decreased siR6611 and increased SUS4 transcript
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SUS4 is upregulated in tt19-8 S2 suppressor seedlings. 2. A small RNA (siR6611) predicted to target SUS4 is decreased in suppressor lines. 3. SUS4 activation provides a plausible mechanism linking disabled small-RNA pathways to increased sucrose consumption and carbon flow into flavonoids.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: higher in tt19-8 S2; not highly expressed in rdr6 single mutant alone according to comparison with published data
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: siR6611; PPR gene At1g62930 (indirect regulatory source)
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: anthocyanin inductive conditions; high sucrose; high light
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Represents a carbon-allocation node that may be manipulated to enhance precursor supply for specialized metabolites, though causal validation remains indirect in this paper.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a metabolism-relevant common gene; direct genetic functional validation was not performed.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: pigments reporting stress and metabolic status
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts leucoanthocyanidins into 3-hydroxyanthocyanidins involved in anthocyanin formation.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate precursor/direct product for anthocyanin formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of ANS/TDS4 abolishes anthocyanins; in combination with rdr6 strongly increases total flavonoid accumulation and carbon flux into the flavonoid pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: double mutant analysis (tds4-4 rdr6-15); flavonoid aglycone LC-MS/MS profiling; anthocyanin quantification
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. tds4-4, a null allele of ANS/TDS4, blocks anthocyanin accumulation. 2. The tds4-4 rdr6-15 double mutant shows total flavonoid levels comparable to tt19-8 rdr6-15 and about threefold higher than wild type. 3. This demonstrates that the synergistic carbon-push effect with rdr6 is not unique to TT19 loss but also occurs with another anthocyanin-pathway defect.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: anthocyanin inductive conditions (3% sucrose, strong/continuous light)
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: metabolomics/LC-MS/MS; RNA-seq GSE136680; small RNA-seq GSE136901; whole-genome resequencing PRJNA562949
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss-of-function eliminates anthocyanin accumulation in single mutant.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Highlights that combining manipulation of siRNA regulation with anthocyanin-branch defects can redirect carbon into flavonoids, but ANS loss itself abolishes anthocyanin production and is unsuitable when pigment accumulation is desired.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The synergistic effect appears linked to anthocyanin-branch dysfunction rather than general flavonoid absence.

Regulation_Genes.Regulator_Type: small RNA biogenesis factor / RNA-dependent RNA polymerase
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: pigments reporting stress and metabolic status
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: post-transcriptional gene silencing via tasiRNA/siRNA biogenesis
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: TAS4-derived siRNAs affecting PAP1; PAP2; MYB113; TAS3 target ARF4; indirectly SUS4 via siR6611 pathway
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes production of tasiRNAs/siRNAs that repress PAP1/PAP2 and other targets; loss of RDR6 derepresses anthocyanin and carbohydrate metabolism genes.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: central carbon metabolism homeostasis and flavonoid biosynthesis flux
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Homeostatic small-RNA-mediated regulation inferred; not fully established
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Functions in the RDR6-SGS3-DCL4 small RNA system
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: rdr6 mutations suppress tt19 anthocyanin deficiency and, together with flavonoid-pathway dysfunction, push 3- to 5-fold more carbon into flavonoid biosynthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller; loss of RDR6 is decisive for exposing hidden carbon-push regulation when combined with tt19 or tds4 defects.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: EMS suppressor mapping; allele validation with rdr6-11 and rdr6-15; whole-genome resequencing; RNA-seq; small RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; metabolite profiling; double mutant analysis
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Four independent tt19 suppressor alleles mapped to RDR6. 2. rdr6 single mutants accumulate more anthocyanins than wild type, but rdr6 combined with tt19 restores anthocyanin accumulation despite loss of TT19. 3. rdr6 with tt19 or tds4 causes a large carbon push into flavonoid biosynthesis, unlike PAP1 overexpression alone. 4. Loss of RDR6 abolishes 21-nt tasiRNA biogenesis and elevates PAP1/PAP2 and other targets.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: anthocyanin inductive conditions; high sucrose; high light; water-grown high-light seedlings
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq GSE136680; small RNA-seq GSE136901; whole-genome resequencing PRJNA562949; untargeted metabolomics
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Alone has modest phenotype, but disabling this major silencing pathway causes global gene expression changes.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential lever to redirect carbon into flavonoids through small RNA pathway engineering, but broad pleiotropic transcriptome effects warrant caution.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: S1, S2, S4, and S8 are independent suppressor alleles; S4 missense allele showed transcriptomic effects different from nonsense alleles.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: pigments reporting stress and metabolic status
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: post-transcriptional gene silencing via tasiRNA/siRNA biogenesis
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: TAS4-derived siRNAs affecting PAP1; PAP2; MYB113
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Required for tasiRNA production that represses anthocyanin regulators; loss derepresses PAP1 and promotes carbon redirection into flavonoids in tt19 background.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: flavonoid biosynthesis flux and central carbon metabolism homeostasis
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Functions in the RDR6-SGS3-DCL4 small RNA system
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: sgs3 mutations suppress tt19 anthocyanin deficiency and enhance flavonoid accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller in the siRNA pathway affecting anthocyanin accumulation when flavonoid sequestration/biosynthesis is perturbed.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: EMS suppressor mapping; RNA-seq-confirmed splice defect; validation with sgs3-11 and sgs3-14 alleles; anthocyanin quantification
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Three independent suppressors (S3/S5/S6) carried the same SGS3 splice-site mutation. 2. Homozygous tt19 sgs3 double mutants accumulate anthocyanins under AIC. 3. SGS3 loss phenocopies other RDR6-SGS3-DCL4 pathway defects in restoring pigmentation and increasing anthocyanin accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: anthocyanin inductive conditions; high sucrose; high light
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq GSE136680; small RNA-seq GSE136901; whole-genome resequencing PRJNA562949
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely broad pleiotropic effects due to loss of small RNA silencing.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Could be exploited to enhance flavonoid flux, but broad off-target regulatory consequences make direct deployment challenging.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Suppressor mutation causes alternative splicing with deletion of five amino acids.

Regulation_Genes.Regulator_Type: small RNA biogenesis factor / Dicer-like ribonuclease
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: pigments reporting stress and metabolic status
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: post-transcriptional gene silencing via processing of 21-nt tasiRNAs/siRNAs
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: TAS4-derived siRNAs affecting PAP1; PAP2; MYB113
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Processes tasiRNAs that repress anthocyanin regulators; loss elevates PAP1/PAP2 and contributes to metabolic carbon push in tt19 background.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: flavonoid biosynthesis flux and central metabolism regulation
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Functions in the RDR6-SGS3-DCL4 small RNA system
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: dcl4 mutations suppress the anthocyanin defect of tt19 and increase anthocyanin accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller in the small RNA pathway required to limit anthocyanin accumulation under flavonoid pathway dysfunction.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: EMS suppressor mapping; RNA-seq-confirmed splice defect; validation with dcl4-2e and dcl4-2t alleles; anthocyanin quantification
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Suppressor S7 mapped to DCL4 splice-site mutation. 2. dcl4 reference alleles confirmed suppression of tt19 anthocyanin deficiency. 3. DCL4 loss, like rdr6 and sgs3 loss, uncovers increased carbon allocation to flavonoid biosynthesis when combined with tt19 dysfunction.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: anthocyanin inductive conditions; high sucrose; high light
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq GSE136680; small RNA-seq GSE136901; whole-genome resequencing PRJNA562949
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely pleiotropic due to perturbation of small RNA production.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate regulatory node to manipulate flavonoid flux, but deployment would require minimizing broad developmental and transcriptomic side effects.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Suppressor mutation abolishes a splice donor site in intron 17.
